questions.scenario: You just SSH'd into Reactor Block A's primary server. First thing: figure out where you are in the filesystem.
questions.question: Print your current working directory.
questions.description: pwd (print working directory) tells you exactly where you are in the filesystem. Always run it first when you SSH into an unfamiliar machine.
use_cases: Verify your script is running from the right directory

questions.scenario: You need to see all files in your home directory, including hidden ones, with their permissions and ownership.
questions.question: List all files (including hidden) in the current directory with long format.
questions.description: ls -la shows all files (including dotfiles starting with .) with permissions, ownership, size, and modification date. The -l flag gives long format, -a shows hidden files.
use_cases: Verify a script was created with correct permissions
hints: ls has flags to show hidden files and long format
Hidden files start with a dot — you need the -a flag

questions.scenario: A backup script created files somewhere under /home/student. You need to find all shell scripts (.sh files) recursively.
questions.question: Find all files ending in .sh under /home/student.
questions.description: find searches directory trees. The -name flag accepts glob patterns. Always quote the pattern to prevent shell expansion before find sees it.
use_cases: Locate scripts scattered across a directory tree
Audit all config files of a specific type
hints: The command is 'find', takes a starting path and a -name filter

questions.scenario: You need to create a nested directory structure /tmp/reactor/logs/archive in one command.
questions.question: Create the directory /tmp/reactor/logs/archive including all parent directories.
questions.description: mkdir -p creates a directory and all missing parent directories in one shot. Without -p, mkdir fails if the parent doesn't exist.
use_cases: Set up log directory structure in scripts
hints: mkdir has a flag to create parent directories automatically

questions.scenario: You have a variable TARGET_DIR that could be empty. Before running rm -rf on it, you must guard against the empty case.
questions.question: Write a guard that exits with an error if TARGET_DIR is empty, then remove it. Set TARGET_DIR=/tmp/reactor first.
questions.description: The pattern [[ -n "$var" ]] || { echo error; exit 1; } is the standard guard before destructive operations. An empty variable in rm -rf $var/* deletes everything in the current directory.
use_cases: Guard before any rm -rf in scripts
Prevent catastrophic mistakes from unset variables
hints: Run: TARGET_DIR=/tmp/reactor && [[ -n "$TARGET_DIR" ]] || { echo 'ERROR: empty'; exit 1; } && rm -rf "$TARGET_DIR"
